title: Infinitesimal Generator
properties: $\mathcal{A}f = \lim_{t \downarrow 0} (P_t f - f)/t$ where $P_t f(x) = \mathbb{E}[f(X_t) \mid X_0 = x]$.
For $dX = b\,dt + \sigma\,dW$: $\;\mathcal{A}f = b \cdot \nabla f + \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{tr}(a D^2 f)$, $\;a = \sigma\sigma^{\mathsf{T}}$.
Brownian motion $\leftrightarrow$ $\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta$; Markov chain $\leftrightarrow$ rate matrix $Q$.
motivation: A Markov process is described globally by its transition semigroup $P_t f(x) = \mathbb{E}[f(X_t) \mid X_0 = x]$, but semigroups are unwieldy objects. The generator is their derivative at $t=0$: a single (differential) operator that encodes the entire local behaviour of the process, in the same way a vector field encodes a flow.
definition: The generator of a Markov semigroup $(P_t)$ is $\mathcal{A}f = \lim_{t \downarrow 0} \frac{P_t f - f}{t}$, defined on the domain of functions where the limit exists (uniformly, or pointwise with bounds). For the Itô diffusion $dX = b\,dt + \sigma\,dW$ and $f \in C_c^2$, $$\mathcal{A}f(x) = \sum_i b_i(x)\,\partial_i f(x) + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} (\sigma\sigma^{\mathsf{T}})_{ij}(x)\,\partial_i\partial_j f(x),$$ a second-order elliptic operator whose first-order part is the drift and whose second-order part is the covariance of the noise.
examples: Brownian motion: $\mathcal{A} = \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta$ — the reason the Laplacian rules both potential theory and diffusion.
Ornstein–Uhlenbeck: $\mathcal{A}f = -\theta x f' + \tfrac{1}{2}\Sigma^2 f''$, self-adjoint in $L^2$ of its Gaussian stationary measure.
A continuous-time Markov chain: $\mathcal{A}$ is the rate matrix $Q$ — the same concept with sums instead of derivatives.
consequences: Dynkin's formula: $\mathbb{E}[f(X_\tau)] = f(x) + \mathbb{E}\!\int_0^\tau \mathcal{A}f(X_s)\,ds$ for nice stopping times — the probabilistic fundamental theorem of calculus.
Kolmogorov equations: $\partial_t P_t f = \mathcal{A} P_t f = P_t \mathcal{A} f$; Feynman–Kac perturbs this to the semigroup $e^{t(\mathcal{A}-V)}$.
$f(X_t) - \int_0^t \mathcal{A}f(X_s)\,ds$ is a martingale for $f$ in the domain; requiring exactly this is the Stroock–Varadhan martingale-problem characterisation of the process.
